entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1329               Post-confirmation Modification

entries.title: Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3015       Plans
entries.content: Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3015       Plans

entries.title: 2.	LIQUIDATION ANALYSIS
entries.content: 2.	LIQUIDATION ANALYSIS

entries.title: The court may only confirm a plan if the value, as...
entries.content: The court may only confirm a plan if the value, as of the effective date of the plan, of property to be distributed under the plan on account of each allowed unsecured claim is not less than the amount that would be paid on such claim if the estate of the debtor were liquidated under chapter 7 on such date.  The standing trustee must perform a liquidation analysis of real and personal property prior to confirmation or modification of a plan.  11 U.S.C. § 1325(a)(4).  Liquidation value is determined by subtracting allowed exemptions and claims secured by the debtor’s property, along with expenses allowed under § 506 of the Bankruptcy Code, from the value of the debtor’s property.  Depending upon controlling law in the jurisdiction, hypothetical costs of sale and capital gains tax may also be deducted.  The standing trustee may base the liquidation analysis on property values disclosed by the debtor in the schedules and other documents, on values the standing trustee anticipates may be proven at the confirmation hearing, or on values obtained by testimony at the meeting of creditors.

entries.title: If a potential preferential or fraudulent transfer...
entries.content: If a potential preferential or fraudulent transfer has been disclosed or is discovered, the standing trustee must include the anticipated chapter 7 recovery as part of the value of the chapter 13 estate.  Denial of improper claims of exemption and invalid or unperfected liens also must be considered. Plan payments to unsecured creditors must equal or exceed the amount they would receive in a chapter 7 liquidation.  11 U.S.C. § 1325(a)(4).

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 1325(a)(4)		Liquidation Analysis
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1325(a)(4)		Liquidation Analysis

entries.title: 3.  	DISPOSABLE INCOME TEST
entries.content: 3.  	DISPOSABLE INCOME TEST

entries.title: The standing trustee must be familiar with control...
entries.content: The standing trustee must be familiar with controlling law and precedent in the jurisdiction defining disposable income and allowable expenses.  The standing trustee must review Official Form 22C (Chapter 13 Statement of Current Monthly Income and Calculation of Commitment Period and Disposable Income) and other information submitted b[py the debtor to determine if the plan meets the requirements of the disposable income test, and object as appropriate if the debtor does not commit all projected disposable income toward plan payments to unsecured creditors.  11 U.S.C. § 1325(b).

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 1325(b)		Disposable Income Test
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1325(b)		Disposable Income Test

entries.title: 4.	FEASIBILITY TEST
entries.content: 4.	FEASIBILITY TEST

entries.title: The court is required to determine that the debtor...
entries.content: The court is required to determine that the debtor will be able to make all payments proposed under the plan and to comply with the plan. The standing trustee must make an independent judgment regarding feasibility, based upon the information provided by the debtor in the schedules and other documents, and object as appropriate under controlling law and precedent in the jurisdiction.  11 U.S.C. § 1325(a)(6).

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 1325(a)(6)		Feasibility Test
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1325(a)(6)		Feasibility Test

entries.title: Practice Tips:
entries.content: Practice Tips:

entries.title: Filed claims may impact the feasibility of a plan....
entries.content: Filed claims may impact the feasibility of a plan.  Therefore, the standing trustee should make an additional feasibility review after the bar date has passed.

entries.title: D.  	CLAIMS
entries.content: D.  	CLAIMS

entries.title: Summary of Standing Trustee Responsibilities
entries.content: Summary of Standing Trustee Responsibilities

entries.title: 1. 	Review claims promptly and object when claims ...
entries.content: 1. 	Review claims promptly and object when claims are untimely, improperly documented, or improperly classified, or are duplicates.

entries.title: 2.	Not make plan payments to creditors who do not ...
entries.content: 2.	Not make plan payments to creditors who do not file claims, or file untimely claims, except as permitted by controlling law in the jurisdiction.

entries.title: 3.	Verify that claims are properly classified in t...
entries.content: 3.	Verify that claims are properly classified in the plan and permit separate classification only as permitted by controlling law in the jurisdiction.

entries.title: 4.	Allow and make payments on post-petition claims...
entries.content: 4.	Allow and make payments on post-petition claims only as permitted by the Bankruptcy Code or as provided in the plan.

entries.title: 5.	Move for subordination of a claim only in rare ...
entries.content: 5.	Move for subordination of a claim only in rare circumstances.

entries.title: 1.	REVIEW, POSTING, AND OBJECTION - GENERALLY
entries.content: 1.	REVIEW, POSTING, AND OBJECTION - GENERALLY

entries.title: The Bankruptcy Code requires the standing trustee ...
entries.content: The Bankruptcy Code requires the standing trustee to examine proofs of claim and object to the allowance of any claim that is improper if a purpose would be served.  Accordingly, the standing trustee must verify that claims are proper with respect to timeliness of filing, dollar amount and supporting documentation.  The standing trustee must review the actual proofs of claim and make an independent determination of classification and amount of the claim.  The standing trustee also must review, and if appropriate, object to nonpriority unsecured claims if the plan proposes a distribution to nonpriority unsecured creditors.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(5), 11 U.S.C. §§ 506, 1322.

entries.title: Some of the issues a standing trustee must conside...
entries.content: Some of the issues a standing trustee must consider when reviewing claims are:

entries.title: The standing trustee must have a procedure in plac...
entries.content: The standing trustee must have a procedure in place to identify duplicate claims and to object or take other appropriate action.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(5).

entries.title: b.	The standing trustee must verify that the prope...
entries.content: b.	The standing trustee must verify that the proper interest rate is paid on claims. The standing trustee must be familiar with the requirements of the Bankruptcy Code and applicable decisions in the district regarding the payment of interest.  11 U.S.C. §§ 506(b), 511.

entries.title: c.	The standing trustee must review schedules and ...
entries.content: c.	The standing trustee must review schedules and compare liens and judgments listed in the schedules and plan to claims that are

entries.title: filed, and take appropriate action to reconcile an...
entries.content: filed, and take appropriate action to reconcile any discrepancies. 11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(4).

entries.title: d.	The standing trustee must review the claims fil...
entries.content: d.	The standing trustee must review the claims filed by creditors who have received pre-confirmation adequate protection payments to verify that the claim amount has been reduced by any amount paid by the debtor before confirmation.  11 U.S.C. § 1326(a)(1)(C).

entries.title: e.	The standing trustee must review secured and un...
entries.content: e.	The standing trustee must review secured and unsecured claims for appropriate documentation, accuracy and timeliness.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(5).

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 502(b)		Allowance of Claims
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 502(b)		Allowance of Claims

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1)		Duty to Review Claims (§ 7...
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1)		Duty to Review Claims (§ 704(a)(5))

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 1326(a)(1)(C)	Payments
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1326(a)(1)(C)	Payments

entries.title: Practice Tips:
entries.content: Practice Tips:

entries.title: 1.		The standing trustee should review claims to d...
entries.content: 1.		The standing trustee should review claims to determine whether the secured creditor’s lien is subject to avoidance pursuant to sections 544 through 549.  The standing trustee also should determine whether attorney fees and costs added to the claim are reasonable.

entries.title: 2.		The trustee should be aware of the controlling...
entries.content: 2.		The trustee should be aware of the controlling authority in the jurisdiction as to the requirement that secured creditors file proofs of claim.  Even if secured claims are not required to be filed, there may be circumstances where the trustee should obtain and review the security documents to ensure that a claim is secured before paying such claim in the plan. The standing trustee also should review claims of creditors seeking adequate protection payments.

entries.title: 3.		If a claim is filed as secured by reason of a ...
entries.content: 3.		If a claim is filed as secured by reason of a judgment, the standing trustee should verify that the judgment was properly recorded at least 90 days prior to the filing.

entries.title: 4.		The standing trustee should verify the validit...
entries.content: 4.		The standing trustee should verify the validity and classification of tax claims.

entries.title: 2.	REVIEW, POSTING, AND OBJECTION - MORTGAGE PROOF...
entries.content: 2.	REVIEW, POSTING, AND OBJECTION - MORTGAGE PROOFS OF 	CLAIM

entries.title: For claims secured by a lien on the debtor’s resid...
entries.content: For claims secured by a lien on the debtor’s residence that are to

entries.title: be paid by the trustee under the plan, the trustee...
entries.content: be paid by the trustee under the plan, the trustee must, at a

entries.title: a.	Verify that copies of documents supporting a pe...
entries.content: a.	Verify that copies of documents supporting a perfected security interest are attached to the proof of claim.  If the secured creditor/mortgagee fails to attach copies of documents supporting a perfected security interest and the trustee is unable to confirm that the creditor/mortgagee has perfected its security interest, the trustee must take appropriate action.  11 U.S.C. §§  506, 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(5), Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3001.

entries.title: b.	Verify that there is an itemization of the pre-...
entries.content: b.	Verify that there is an itemization of the pre-petition fees, costs, and other charges attached to the proof of claim.  If the proof of claim does not include an itemization of the fees, costs, and other charges and the trustee is unable to obtain the itemization, the trustee must take appropriate action.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(5), Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3001.

entries.title: c.	Verify whether the proof of claim includes a fl...
entries.content: c.	Verify whether the proof of claim includes a flat fee for review of the chapter 13 plan prior to confirmation and for preparation of the proof of claim and, if so, whether the fee is reasonable and fairly reflects the attorney’s fee incurred.  If the trustee determines that the fee is not reasonable, the trustee must object to the claim or take such other action as may be appropriate.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(5), Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3001.

entries.title: d.	Verify that a secured proof of claim has been f...
entries.content: d.	Verify that a secured proof of claim has been filed. If no claim has been filed, the trustee must ensure that the mortgagee’s claim is not paid or take other action as may be appropriate.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(5).

entries.title: e. 	If a trustee determines, based on the trustee’...
entries.content: e. 	If a trustee determines, based on the trustee’s facial review of the mortgage proof of claim, that the fees, costs, or charges are material and improper, the trustee must take appropriate action.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(5), Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3001.

entries.title: 3.	UNTIMELY CLAIMS
entries.content: 3.	UNTIMELY CLAIMS

entries.title: An unsecured creditor must file a proof of claim b...
entries.content: An unsecured creditor must file a proof of claim by the claims bar date to be eligible to receive payment.  The standing trustee may not make payment under a plan to an unsecured creditor that did not file a timely proof of claim.  11 U.S.C. § 502(b)(9), Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3002(c).  Courts are divided as to whether a secured creditor must file a proof of claim.  Therefore, the standing trustee must follow controlling legal authority in the jurisdiction as to payment of secured creditors who have not filed claims.

entries.title: If the creditor does not timely file a proof of cl...
entries.content: If the creditor does not timely file a proof of claim, the standing trustee or debtor may file a claim within 30 days after the expiration of the bar date.  In most circumstances, the standing trustee does not have sufficient information regarding a creditor’s claim against the debtor, and the standing trustee should not file a claim on behalf of a creditor.  As an alternative, the standing trustee may provide notice regarding claims that will alert the debtor to consider filing claims for non-filing creditors.

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C § 502 (b)(9)  		Untimely Claims, Tax Clai...
entries.content: 11 U.S.C § 502 (b)(9)  		Untimely Claims, Tax Claims

entries.title: Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3001		Proof of Claim
entries.content: Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3001		Proof of Claim

entries.title: Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3002		Filing Proof of Claim or I...
entries.content: Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3002		Filing Proof of Claim or Interest

entries.title: Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3004		Filing Claims by Debtor or...
entries.content: Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3004		Filing Claims by Debtor or Trustee

entries.title: 4.	CLASSIFICATION OF CLAIMS
entries.content: 4.	CLASSIFICATION OF CLAIMS

entries.title: The standing trustee must be familiar with control...
entries.content: The standing trustee must be familiar with controlling law and precedent in the jurisdiction regarding classification of claims and object to such classification if a purpose would be served.

entries.title: 5.	POST-PETITION CLAIMS
entries.content: 5.	POST-PETITION CLAIMS

entries.title: The Bankruptcy Code allows an entity to file a pro...
entries.content: The Bankruptcy Code allows an entity to file a proof of claim for certain types of post-petition debts, such as contract rejection damage claims, post-petition tax claims or certain consumer claims (of the kind described in 11 U.S.C. § 1305).  If such a proof of claim is filed and it is an allowed claim, it will be treated as a pre-petition claim.  The debtor may provide for payment of these post-petition tax and/or consumer claims in the plan, but unlike other claims, if holders of these claims do not file a proof of claim, the debtor or the standing trustee may not file a claim on behalf of the claimholder.  The standing trustee must not make plan payments on account of these claims if no proof of claim has been filed or the debtor does not provide for payment of the claim in the plan.

entries.title: In addition, unless the plan provides otherwise, t...
entries.content: In addition, unless the plan provides otherwise, the standing trustee must object to a post-petition consumer claim if the holder of the claim knew or should have known that prior approval by the standing trustee was practicable and was not obtained. 11 U.S.C §§ 1305, 1322(b)(6).

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 1305       		Post-petition Claims
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1305       		Post-petition Claims

entries.title: 6.	SUBORDINATION OF CLAIMS
entries.content: 6.	SUBORDINATION OF CLAIMS

entries.title: The standing trustee may obtain a court order subo...
entries.content: The standing trustee may obtain a court order subordinating certain claims to other claims for purposes of distribution.  Subordination may be based upon an agreement, or upon principles of equitable subordination.  Generally, equitable subordination requires misconduct on the part of the creditor that has injured the debtor or conferred an unfair advantage to the creditor.  The standing trustee should bring motions for subordination only in rare circumstances when the general body of creditors would benefit, the legal grounds for subordination are clear, and it would be difficult for the debtor or another creditor to bring the action.

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 510          		Subordination of Claims
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 510          		Subordination of Claims

entries.title: E.  	EMPLOYMENT OF ATTORNEYS AND OTHER PROFESSIONA...
entries.content: E.  	EMPLOYMENT OF ATTORNEYS AND OTHER PROFESSIONALS

entries.title: Summary of Standing Trustee Duties
entries.content: Summary of Standing Trustee Duties

entries.title: A standing trustee may employ outside attorneys or...
entries.content: A standing trustee may employ outside attorneys or other professionals as an expense of the trust only upon approval of the United States Trustee.

entries.title: Legal defense of a standing trustee or employee of...
entries.content: Legal defense of a standing trustee or employee of a standing trustee personally as an expense of the trust operation is subject to a determination by the United States Trustee that the conduct complained of is within the scope of the standing trustee’s official duties and that defense of the allegation is an actual and necessary expense of the trust operation.

entries.title: 1.	EMPLOYMENT OF PROFESSIONALS
entries.content: 1.	EMPLOYMENT OF PROFESSIONALS

entries.title: The standing trustee may employ an outside attorne...
entries.content: The standing trustee may employ an outside attorney, consultant, or other professional as an expense of the trust operation only upon approval of the United States Trustee.  A request for approval must include the amount, terms and conditions of employment, a copy of any proposed written agreement, an explanation as to why the services are not capable of being handled by staff employees, and, if applicable, the reason the expense would not more appropriately be borne by an individual estate through service of a professional employed under 11 U.S.C. § 327.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(2). A standing trustee seeking to employ outside counsel may redact from any written agreement submitted to the United States Trustee, any information regarding the legal theories or assessments of the case. Disclosure in a standing trustee annual or amended budget is not considered a request for approval of employment of an outside professional.

entries.title: 2.	LEGAL DEFENSE
entries.content: 2.	LEGAL DEFENSE

entries.title: When the standing trustee requests approval to emp...
entries.content: When the standing trustee requests approval to employ an attorney to defend the standing trustee or an employee personally as an expense of the trust operation, the request needs to contain the following additional information: (a) a detailed summary of the facts surrounding the challenged conduct, (b) copies of relevant documents, and (c) a budget describing the legal services to be performed with the anticipated cost of the services.

entries.title: A standing trustee employing an attorney as an exp...
entries.content: A standing trustee employing an attorney as an expense of the trust operation to defend the standing trustee or an employee personally will be required to submit to the United States Trustee a quarterly status report of the standing trustee or trustee’s counsel.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(2), 28 U.S.C. § 586(b).  Continuing approval of the employment is contingent on the United States Trustee’s determination that the conduct complained of appears to have been performed within the scope of the standing trustee’s official duties, and that defense of the allegation is an actual and necessary expense of the trust operation.

entries.title: Defense or prosecution of an action by or against ...
entries.content: Defense or prosecution of an action by or against the United States Trustee is personal to the standing trustee and does not constitute an actual and necessary expense of administering a standing trustee’s cases.

entries.title: F.	PLAN PAYMENTS
entries.content: F.	PLAN PAYMENTS

entries.title: The standing trustee must have in place procedures...
entries.content: The standing trustee must have in place procedures to verify that debtors timely commence plan payments.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(5).  In most cases the standing trustee will receive plan payments directly from the debtor or from the debtor’s employer via a wage order.

entries.title: The standing trustee must also have in place contr...
entries.content: The standing trustee must also have in place controls to identify cases in which the debtor is delinquent in plan payments.  Standing trustees can monitor case delinquencies through the use of periodic reports.  The standing trustee must move for dismissal or conversion or take other appropriate action throughout the life of a case in which the debtor is significantly delinquent in payments.  11 U.S.C. § 1307(c).

entries.title: Early and accurate identification of problem cases...
entries.content: Early and accurate identification of problem cases and prompt action by the standing trustee is essential.

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 1326(a)       Commencement of Plan Pay...
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1326(a)       Commencement of Plan Payments

entries.title: Practice Tips:
entries.content: Practice Tips:

entries.title: 1.		Wage Orders:  A debtor is more likely to succe...
entries.content: 1.		Wage Orders:  A debtor is more likely to successfully complete a plan if payments are made through voluntary wage orders.  The standing trustee should encourage voluntary wage orders in all cases where appropriate.  If necessary, the cooperation of debtor’s counsel should be sought in encouraging voluntary wage orders.  In any case in which the debtor fails to make any of the scheduled payments on time, the standing trustee may seek a voluntary wage order as a condition for not filing a motion to dismiss or for withdrawal of a pending motion to dismiss.

entries.title: 2. 		Monthly Delinquency Reports:  Most standing t...
entries.content: 2. 		Monthly Delinquency Reports:  Most standing trustees generate monthly reports that list cases in which payments are delinquent.  The criteria for inclusion on the reports vary widely.  Standing trustees should establish a monthly report, and should establish criteria which bring to the standing trustee’s attention debtors who are either substantially delinquent in dollar amount or substantially delinquent in number of payments.  See Chapter 4 for more information about these reports.

entries.title: G.	CHAPTER 13 DEBTORS ENGAGED IN BUSINESS
entries.content: G.	CHAPTER 13 DEBTORS ENGAGED IN BUSINESS

entries.title: Summary of Standing Trustee Duties
entries.content: Summary of Standing Trustee Duties

entries.title: Establish a procedure for the prompt recognition o...
entries.content: Establish a procedure for the prompt recognition of a business case.

entries.title: Investigate and report on business case as require...
entries.content: Investigate and report on business case as required by the Bankruptcy Code and Rules.

entries.title: Monitor the business debtor’s periodic reports, an...
entries.content: Monitor the business debtor’s periodic reports, and take action where appropriate.

entries.title: 1.	IDENTIFYING A CHAPTER 13 BUSINESS CASE
entries.content: 1.	IDENTIFYING A CHAPTER 13 BUSINESS CASE

entries.title: The effectiveness of the standing trustee’s treatm...
entries.content: The effectiveness of the standing trustee’s treatment of the business case is dependent upon the standing trustee’s ability to make early identification of the potential business case.  The Bankruptcy Code defines a chapter 13 debtor engaged in business as “[a] debtor that is self-employed and incurs trade credit in the production of income from such employment...”  However, it is often not possible to determine from the petition and schedules whether a case fits within that definition.  Further, a business debtor may incur trade credit after filing.  Therefore, the standing trustee must develop and apply a list of objective criteria to determine which cases to classify as business cases.  11 U.S.C. §§ 1302(c) and 1304.

entries.title: At a minimum, the standing trustee must consider a...
entries.content: At a minimum, the standing trustee must consider as a business debtor a debtor with employees or a debtor incurring significant post-petition unsecured business debt.

entries.title: When the standing trustee’s analysis indicates tha...
entries.content: When the standing trustee’s analysis indicates that a chapter 13 filing may be a business case, the standing trustee should inquire further to determine whether the debtor is actually engaged in business.  Some standing trustees conduct this inquiry at a meeting prior to the meeting of creditors; others request that the debtor bring documents such as tax returns, financial statements and the like to the meeting of creditors and examine the debtor at that time.

entries.title: Some factors, which may indicate that a chapter 13...
entries.content: Some factors, which may indicate that a chapter 13 debtor is engaged 	in business, include the following:

entries.title: 1.	The use of the term “doing business as,” “d/b/a...
entries.content: 1.	The use of the term “doing business as,” “d/b/a” or "dba" in the

entries.title: style of the case or as part of the debtor’s name;
entries.content: style of the case or as part of the debtor’s name;

entries.title: 2.	Designation on the petition as a business;
entries.content: 2.	Designation on the petition as a business;

entries.title: 3.	Debtor is self-employed;
entries.content: 3.	Debtor is self-employed;

entries.title: 4.	Debtor is incurring trade credit in the product...
entries.content: 4.	Debtor is incurring trade credit in the production of income;

entries.title: 5.	Debtor has one or more employees;
entries.content: 5.	Debtor has one or more employees;

entries.title: 6.	Debtor generates business income;
entries.content: 6.	Debtor generates business income;

entries.title: 7.	The existence of inventory for manufacture and/...
entries.content: 7.	The existence of inventory for manufacture and/or resale;

entries.title: 8.	The existence of trade debt and/or receivables;
entries.content: 8.	The existence of trade debt and/or receivables;

entries.title: 9.	Ownership and/or lease of business property;
entries.content: 9.	Ownership and/or lease of business property;

entries.title: 10.	Ownership of rental property;
entries.content: 10.	Ownership of rental property;

entries.title: 11.	Filing of Schedule C or Schedule E with tax re...
entries.content: 11.	Filing of Schedule C or Schedule E with tax return;

entries.title: 12.	Ownership and/or lease of office equipment, fu...
entries.content: 12.	Ownership and/or lease of office equipment, furnishings, and

entries.title: 13.	Obligations for payroll taxes, business tax, r...
entries.content: 13.	Obligations for payroll taxes, business tax, real estate tax and

entries.title: personalty tax;
entries.content: personalty tax;

entries.title: 14.	Obligation for business related insurance;
entries.content: 14.	Obligation for business related insurance;

entries.title: 15.	Schedules I and J include business income and ...
entries.content: 15.	Schedules I and J include business income and expenses; and

entries.title: 16.	Business expenses deducted from income to dete...
entries.content: 16.	Business expenses deducted from income to determine

entries.title: “disposable income.”
entries.content: “disposable income.”

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 1304		Debtors Engaged in Business
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1304		Debtors Engaged in Business

entries.title: 2.	INVESTIGATE AND REPORT	 ON BUSINESS CASE
entries.content: 2.	INVESTIGATE AND REPORT	 ON BUSINESS CASE

entries.title: If the debtor is engaged in business, the standing...
entries.content: If the debtor is engaged in business, the standing trustee must perform certain investigative duties in addition to those performed in a non-business case.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(c).

entries.title: a.	Standing Trustee’s Investigation of a Debtor En...
entries.content: a.	Standing Trustee’s Investigation of a Debtor Engaged in Business

entries.title: The standing trustee’s investigation of a debtor e...
entries.content: The standing trustee’s investigation of a debtor engaged in business may vary depending upon the nature and size of the business.

entries.title: The standing trustee must meet with the debtor and...
entries.content: The standing trustee must meet with the debtor and debtor’s counsel prior to or at the meeting of creditors to examine and review the debtor’s business documents.  The standing trustee must direct the debtor to bring to the meeting of creditors documents reflecting the financial condition of the debtor’s business.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(c), Fed. R. Bankr. P. 2004.

entries.title: At the meeting of creditors, the standing trustee ...
entries.content: At the meeting of creditors, the standing trustee must advise the debtor of the debtor’s duty under Bankruptcy Rule 2015 to keep records, make reports and give notice of the case.  11 U.S.C. § 1304(c).

entries.title: Examples of documents reflecting the business debt...
entries.content: Examples of documents reflecting the business debtor’s financial condition are:

entries.title: 1.	Copies of Federal and State tax returns, along ...
entries.content: 1.	Copies of Federal and State tax returns, along with all supporting schedules, for at least the two years preceding the filing;

entries.title: 2.	Copies of financial statements furnished to a t...
entries.content: 2.	Copies of financial statements furnished to a third party, such 	as a trade creditor or a bank, within the two years preceding the filing of the petition, including but not limited to the balance sheet, income statement and cash flow statement;

entries.title: 3.	Current books and records of the business, incl...
entries.content: 3.	Current books and records of the business, including checks and 	check registers;

entries.title: 4.	Monthly profit and loss statements for at least...
entries.content: 4.	Monthly profit and loss statements for at least the year preceding the filing;

entries.title: 5.	Current schedule of accounts receivable and acc...
entries.content: 5.	Current schedule of accounts receivable and accounts payable;

entries.title: 6.	Current insurance policies; and
entries.content: 6.	Current insurance policies; and

entries.title: 7.	Lease agreements.
entries.content: 7.	Lease agreements.

entries.content: b.	Contents and Filing of the Investigative Statement or Summary

entries.title: As soon as practicable, the standing trustee shall...
entries.content: As soon as practicable, the standing trustee shall file with the court, and serve upon the United States Trustee, a statement of the standing trustee’s investigation of the debtor’s business operations.  The required statement of investigation may be included in an objection to confirmation or report on confirmation filed with the court.  If the investigation uncovers evidence of fraud, the standing trustee must notify the United States Trustee and take appropriate action.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(c), 28 U.S.C. § 586(a)(3)(F).

entries.content: 11.		Turnover actions, if applicable;

entries.title: 12.  	Any fact pertaining to fraud, dishonesty, in...
entries.content: 12.  	Any fact pertaining to fraud, dishonesty, incompetence, misconduct or irregularity in the management of the affairs of the debtor or to a cause of action available to the estate; and

entries.title: 13.		Except to the extent the court orders otherwi...
entries.content: 13.		Except to the extent the court orders otherwise, a statement concerning the acts, conduct, assets, liabilities, and financial condition of the debtor, the operation of the debtor’s business and the desirability of the continuance of such business, and any other matter relevant to the case or to the formulation of a plan.

entries.title: 3.	PERIODIC MONITORING
entries.content: 3.	PERIODIC MONITORING

entries.title: The debtor must file periodic reports required by ...
entries.content: The debtor must file periodic reports required by the Bankruptcy Code and local rules or practices.  11 U.S.C. § 1304, Fed. R. Bankr. P. 2015.  The standing trustee must review the periodic reports to verify that the ongoing business, while in bankruptcy, does not fall in deeper financial difficulty than at the time of the filing of the case.   The level of monitoring is dependent upon the nature of the case and the business expertise of the debtor.

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 1304       	Debtor Engaged in Business...
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1304       	Debtor Engaged in Business (adopting § 704(a)(8))

entries.title: Practice Tips:
entries.content: Practice Tips:

entries.title: Monitoring might include the debtor meeting with t...
entries.content: Monitoring might include the debtor meeting with the standing trustee’s business case analyst, if applicable, to review the budget, an evaluation of the debtor’s accounting systems, an on-site tour of the business premises, the requirement that periodic operating reports be filed along with bank statements, tax deposits and payment forms, and monitoring of insurance coverage.

entries.title: H.	DISBURSEMENTS
entries.content: H.	DISBURSEMENTS

entries.title: Summary of Standing Trustee Duties
entries.content: Summary of Standing Trustee Duties

entries.title: 1.		Disburse a debtor’s plan payments on a monthly...
entries.content: 1.		Disburse a debtor’s plan payments on a monthly basis in accordance with the Bankruptcy Code, and other controlling authority in the jurisdiction.

entries.title: 2.		Properly classify and pay creditors’ claims in...
entries.content: 2.		Properly classify and pay creditors’ claims in accordance with the debtor’s plan.

entries.title: 3.		Detect and recover erroneous disbursements.
entries.content: 3.		Detect and recover erroneous disbursements.

entries.title: 1.	MONTHLY DISBURSEMENTS
entries.content: 1.	MONTHLY DISBURSEMENTS

entries.title: The standing trustee must retain debtor payments o...
entries.content: The standing trustee must retain debtor payments of amounts proposed by the plan until confirmation, unless otherwise ordered by the court or required by law or local rule.  11 U.S.C. § 1326(a)(2).  If the plan is confirmed, the standing trustee must distribute such payments in accordance with the plan as soon as practicable. Id. The standing trustee must have in place procedures designed to assure that disbursements to creditors are made soon after confirmation and the disbursements are made monthly thereafter.

entries.title: If the case is dismissed or converted pre-confirma...
entries.content: If the case is dismissed or converted pre-confirmation, the standing trustee must distribute or return funds as required by 11 U.S.C. § 1326(a)(2), after deducting § 503(b) claims to the extent allowed by court order.  See also Chapter 2, section D, Calculation and Collection of Percentage Fee.  It is the Program’s longstanding position to disfavor awards of administrative expenses to standing trustees pursuant to 11 U.S.C. § 503(b) unless otherwise ordered by the court or required by law or local rule.  Trustees who receive such awards may not also collect a percentage fee in those cases.  If the case is dismissed or converted post-confirmation, the standing trustee must make distributions in accordance with controlling law. [Language revised May 2015.]

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 348		Effect of Conversion
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 348		Effect of Conversion

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 349		Effect of Dismissal
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 349		Effect of Dismissal

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 524(i)	Failure of Creditor to Credit P...
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 524(i)	Failure of Creditor to Credit Payments

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 1326(a)(2) Plan Payments
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1326(a)(2) Plan Payments

entries.title: 28 U.S.C. § 586(e)(2)	Collection of Percentage Fee
entries.content: 28 U.S.C. § 586(e)(2)	Collection of Percentage Fee

entries.title: Practice Tips:
entries.content: Practice Tips:

entries.title: A standing trustee who receives ongoing mortgage p...
entries.content: A standing trustee who receives ongoing mortgage payments should have a procedure in place to verify the status of the mortgage at the end of the bankruptcy.

entries.title: 2.	CLASSIFICATION AND PAYMENT OF CLAIMS
entries.content: 2.	CLASSIFICATION AND PAYMENT OF CLAIMS

entries.title: The standing trustee must have procedures in place...
entries.content: The standing trustee must have procedures in place to properly classify and pay creditors’ claims in accordance with the debtor’s plan. 11 U.S.C. § 1326(c).

entries.title: 3.	ERRONEOUS DISBURSEMENTS
entries.content: 3.	ERRONEOUS DISBURSEMENTS

entries.title: The standing trustee must have a procedure in plac...
entries.content: The standing trustee must have a procedure in place to detect and recover erroneous disbursements.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(2), 28 U.S.C. § 586(b).

entries.title: The standing trustee must first attempt to recover...
entries.content: The standing trustee must first attempt to recover the erroneous disbursement from the party and must have documented reasonable efforts to recover the disbursement. If unsuccessful, the standing trustee must determine whether the erroneous disbursement is less than the deductible on the standing trustee’s errors and omissions policy.  If the amount is less than the deductible, the standing trustee must request authorization from the United States Trustee to use funds from the expense account.  If the amount is greater than the deductible, the standing trustee must consult with the United States Trustee on the appropriate course of action.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(2), 28 U.S.C. § 586(b).

entries.title: I.	EXERCISE OF AVOIDANCE POWERS
entries.content: I.	EXERCISE OF AVOIDANCE POWERS

entries.title: Summary of Standing Trustee Duties
entries.content: Summary of Standing Trustee Duties

entries.title: 1.		Be familiar with the avoidance powers granted ...
entries.content: 1.		Be familiar with the avoidance powers granted to the standing trustee by the Bankruptcy Code.

entries.title: 2.		Pursue avoidance action in appropriate cases, ...
entries.content: 2.		Pursue avoidance action in appropriate cases, or, in the alternative, obtain a tolling agreement preserving the standing trustee’s rights.

entries.title: 1.	AVOIDANCE POWERS
entries.content: 1.	AVOIDANCE POWERS

entries.title: A fundamental goal of the Bankruptcy Code is to en...
entries.content: A fundamental goal of the Bankruptcy Code is to ensure equality of distribution among creditors of the same class. The standing trustee possesses various powers to avoid unequal treatment among creditors of the same class or other parties in interest.  The standing trustee must be familiar with these powers.  11 U.S.C. §§ 544-553.

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. §§ 544 - 553		Trustee Avoidance Powers
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. §§ 544 - 553		Trustee Avoidance Powers

entries.title: 2.	STANDING TRUSTEE RESPONSIBILITY
entries.content: 2.	STANDING TRUSTEE RESPONSIBILITY

entries.title: The standing trustee must exercise business judgme...
entries.content: The standing trustee must exercise business judgment to determine whether it is appropriate to pursue avoidance actions.  11 U.S.C. §§ 544-553.  Courts are divided as to whether the debtor has authority to file avoidance actions.  Furthermore, the debtor may have no incentive to pursue a preference or fraudulent transfer action, as, for example, when the debtor has made transfers to relatives or friends. As an alternative to a formal recovery action, the standing trustee may reach an agreement with the debtor and debtor’s counsel to “cash out” the transfer, by which the debtor agrees that the value of the transfer will be factored into the liquidation analysis and the disposable income test.

entries.title: If the standing trustee “cashes out” such a transf...
entries.content: If the standing trustee “cashes out” such a transfer, the standing trustee should determine whether it is appropriate to obtain a written agreement from the debtor and transferee to toll the statute of limitations relating to any applicable action to recover transfers or avoid liens.  If the “cash out” agreement should fail, the tolling agreement may preserve the standing trustee’s rights to pursue avoidance actions.  In addition, if the case is subsequently converted, the tolling agreement may preserve the rights and powers of a successor trustee to proceed with an avoidance action.

entries.title: Generally, any action brought by the standing trus...
entries.content: Generally, any action brought by the standing trustee to recover money or property pursuant to an avoiding power is brought as an adversary proceeding and governed by Rule 7001.  The standing trustee does not need court approval to pursue such an action.  If it is necessary to employ outside counsel to pursue avoidance actions, employment must be approved by the bankruptcy court pursuant to section 327, with the cost of counsel to be borne by the estate rather than from the standing trustee’s expense account.  11 U.S.C. § 327, 28 U.S.C. § 586(b).

entries.title: Practice Tips:
entries.content: Practice Tips:

entries.title: The standing trustee should determine whether or n...
entries.content: The standing trustee should determine whether or not a tolling agreement is necessary based on the circumstances of each case.  Factors to consider include the value of the assets transferred, the cost and practicality of obtaining a tolling agreement, litigation the action (including any fees and costs incurred by professionals hired to represent the standing trustee), collecting a judgment from the defendant, any defenses to an avoidance action, and whether any portion of the recovery might be subject to an exemption by the debtor.

entries.title: J.	DISCHARGE OF DEBTOR
entries.content: J.	DISCHARGE OF DEBTOR

entries.title: Summary of Standing Trustee Duties
entries.content: Summary of Standing Trustee Duties

entries.title: 1.		Certify promptly to the court when the debtor ...
entries.content: 1.		Certify promptly to the court when the debtor has successfully completed the plan.

entries.title: 2.		Object to an application for a hardship discha...
entries.content: 2.		Object to an application for a hardship discharge in cases where the debtor is not eligible.

entries.title: 3.	    Provide the statutory second notice, if req...
entries.content: 3.	    Provide the statutory second notice, if required, to DSO holders.

entries.title: 1.	ELIGIBILITY FOR DISCHARGE
entries.content: 1.	ELIGIBILITY FOR DISCHARGE

entries.title: The standing trustee must promptly certify to the ...
entries.content: The standing trustee must promptly certify to the court in accordance with local rules or practice when the debtor has successfully completed the plan.  11 U.S.C. § 1328.  This certification can be made before or as a part of the final report.  The standing trustee may rely on the debtor’s certification filed pursuant to section 1328(a) that the debtor has paid all amounts payable under any DSO (this applies only to debtors that have DSOs).  The standing trustee is not responsible for certifying to the court that the debtor has completed a post-filing financial management course.  However, if the debtor has not filed a statement of completion of a personal financial management course in accordance with section 1328(g) and Rule 1007(b)(7), the standing trustee should remind the debtor that the bankruptcy court will not grant a discharge to the debtor without the statement of completion having been prepared on the official form and filed with the court.

entries.title: 2.	HARDSHIP DISCHARGE
entries.content: 2.	HARDSHIP DISCHARGE

entries.title: A debtor may apply for a “hardship” discharge befo...
entries.content: A debtor may apply for a “hardship” discharge before completing payments under the plan, at any time after the confirmation of the plan, but only if:

entries.title: a.		the debtor’s failure to complete such payments...
entries.content: a.		the debtor’s failure to complete such payments is due to circumstances for which the debtor should not justly be held accountable;

entries.title: b.		the value, as of the effective date of the pla...
entries.content: b.		the value, as of the effective date of the plan, of property actually distributed under the plan on account of each allowed unsecured claim is not less than the amount that would have been paid on such claim if the estate of the debtor had been liquidated under chapter 7 of this title on such date; and

entries.title: c.		modification of the plan under § 1329 is not p...
entries.content: c.		modification of the plan under § 1329 is not practicable.

entries.title: A hardship discharge is not as broad as a standard...
entries.content: A hardship discharge is not as broad as a standard chapter 13 discharge; it is equivalent to the discharge received by a chapter 7 debtor.

entries.title: The standing trustee must review applications for ...
entries.content: The standing trustee must review applications for hardship discharges and must object to the debtor’s hardship discharge if the debtor does not qualify under the above statutory standards.  11 U.S.C. § 1328(b).

entries.title: 3.	SECOND NOTICE TO DOMESTIC SUPPORT OBLIGATION HO...
entries.content: 3.	SECOND NOTICE TO DOMESTIC SUPPORT OBLIGATION HOLDERS

entries.title: The standing trustee must send a second notice to ...
entries.content: The standing trustee must send a second notice to a DSO claim holder and the State child support enforcement agency when a discharge is granted. The second notice must contain the following information: the last known address of the debtor, the last known name and address of the debtor’s employer; and the name of each creditor that holds a claim that was not discharged or reaffirmed.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(d).

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 1302(d)		Notices of Domestic Support O...
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1302(d)		Notices of Domestic Support Obligations

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 1328			Chapter 13 Discharge
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1328			Chapter 13 Discharge

entries.title: Fed. R. Bankr. P. 1007(b)(7)	Filing of Statements
entries.content: Fed. R. Bankr. P. 1007(b)(7)	Filing of Statements

entries.title: K.	FINAL REPORT
entries.content: K.	FINAL REPORT

entries.title: The standing trustee must file a final report prom...
entries.content: The standing trustee must file a final report promptly after review of the administration of the case and after all checks have cleared.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(9).  Generally, the final report is to be filed within 150 days of the last disbursement in the case.  The standing trustee must serve the report on the debtor, the debtor’s attorney and the United States Trustee, unless the United States Trustee has requested otherwise.  Fed. R. Bankr. P. 5009.

entries.title: The standing trustee must have procedures in place...
entries.content: The standing trustee must have procedures in place for the review of each case prior to filing the final report to ensure that the case has been administered properly and all claims have been paid according to the plan.  11 U.S.C. § 1326(c).  This review must not be conducted by the same staff member who monitored the file during the course of the case.  28 U.S.C. § 586(b).

entries.title: Subject to Rule 1019, the standing trustee must no...
entries.content: Subject to Rule 1019, the standing trustee must not file a document giving the final accounting of receipts and disbursements and requesting a discharge of the trustee’s duties until all checks relating to the case have cleared the bank.  28 U.S.C. § 586(b).  However, the standing trustee may have in place a procedure that certifies to the court that a debtor has completed payments under the plan before the final accounting is completed.

entries.title: Section 347(a) of the Bankruptcy Code requires the...
entries.content: Section 347(a) of the Bankruptcy Code requires the standing trustee to stop payment on any check remaining unpaid ninety days after the final distribution and pay the unclaimed funds to the court.  11 U.S.C. § 347, Fed. R. Bankr. P. 3011.  In some jurisdictions, the court may allow or require standing trustees to reissue final disbursement checks.  The standing trustee should consult with the United States Trustee before implementing such a procedure.

entries.title: The standing trustee must use the Chapter 13 Stand...
entries.content: The standing trustee must use the Chapter 13 Standing Trustee’s Final Report and Account.  28 U.S.C. § 589b, 28 C.F.R. § 58.7.  The Final Report form is available on the Program website at www.justice.gov/ust.  This Uniform Form cannot be altered.

entries.title: If a closed case has to be re-opened, the standing...
entries.content: If a closed case has to be re-opened, the standing trustee should contact the United States Trustee to discuss options available.

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1)		Final Report (adopting § 7...
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1)		Final Report (adopting § 704(a)(9))

entries.title: Practice Tips:
entries.content: Practice Tips:

entries.title: 1.		If a claim is reassigned, both the initial cre...
entries.content: 1.		If a claim is reassigned, both the initial creditor and the subsequent creditor should be listed on the final report.  This will improve the accuracy of the final report.

entries.title: 2.		The standing trustee may consider serving a co...
entries.content: 2.		The standing trustee may consider serving a copy of the final report on all parties in interest as a further internal control.

entries.title: 3.		The standing trustee may implement procedures ...
entries.content: 3.		The standing trustee may implement procedures to allow issuance of a replacement check to creditors who fail to negotiate their final disbursement check within 90 days of issue. However, if the creditor fails to negotiate the replacement check, the standing trustee must pay the unclaimed funds to the Court.  The standing

entries.title: trustee should have in place procedures to determi...
entries.content: trustee should have in place procedures to determine when unpaid funds must be paid to the Court.

entries.title: FINANCIAL AND OPERATIONAL POLICIES, PROCEDURES AND...
entries.content: FINANCIAL AND OPERATIONAL POLICIES, PROCEDURES AND REPORTING REQUIREMENTS

entries.title: CHAPTER 4 – FINANCIAL AND OPERATIONAL POLICIES, PR...
entries.content: CHAPTER 4 – FINANCIAL AND OPERATIONAL POLICIES, PROCEDURES AND REPORTING REQUIREMENTS

entries.title: The requirements in this chapter are designed to e...
entries.content: The requirements in this chapter are designed to ensure the standing trustee has procedures in place to protect the property the standing trustee receives from the debtor, primarily plan payments.  The general authority for the United States Trustee requirements is 28 U.S.C. § 586(b) and the United States Trustee’s interpretation of 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(2) as made applicable to standing trustees by 11 U.S.C. § 1302(b).

entries.title: A.	BANK ACCOUNTS AND DEPOSIT, INVESTMENT AND DISBU...
entries.content: A.	BANK ACCOUNTS AND DEPOSIT, INVESTMENT AND DISBURSEMENT OF FUNDS

entries.title: The standing trustee must deposit all receipts in ...
entries.content: The standing trustee must deposit all receipts in a banking institution that:

entries.title: a.	is insured by the Federal Deposit Insurance Cor...
entries.content: a.	is insured by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation;

entries.title: b.	complies with the requirements of the Bankruptc...
entries.content: b.	complies with the requirements of the Bankruptcy Code and guidelines of the United States Trustee; and

entries.title: c.	is approved by the United States Trustee to hol...
entries.content: c.	is approved by the United States Trustee to hold deposits of bankruptcy estate accounts./

entries.title: The standing trustee must disclose to the United S...
entries.content: The standing trustee must disclose to the United States Trustee the identity of each banking institution in which the standing trustee has an account.  11 U.S.C. § 345.

entries.title: 2.  	DEPOSIT, INVESTMENT, AND DISBURSEMENT OF FUND...
entries.content: 2.  	DEPOSIT, INVESTMENT, AND DISBURSEMENT OF FUNDS

entries.title: The standing trustee must deposit all funds to the...
entries.content: The standing trustee must deposit all funds to the appropriate bank account promptly upon receipt.  The standing trustee must not deposit

entries.title: any funds received into the standing trustee’s bus...
entries.content: any funds received into the standing trustee’s business, personal, or law firm trust accounts.  11 U.S.C. § 345.

entries.title: All interest-bearing funds held by the standing tr...
entries.content: All interest-bearing funds held by the standing trustee shall yield the maximum reasonable net return, taking into account the safety of the deposit or investment, the services provided by the bank, and other requirements of the Bankruptcy Code and this Handbook.

entries.title: Funds deposited in any standing trustee account ma...
entries.content: Funds deposited in any standing trustee account may not be transferred or withdrawn in cash or for any personal use of the standing trustee or the staff.

entries.title: The standing trustee must assist the United States...
entries.content: The standing trustee must assist the United States Trustee in obtaining bank records, documents and information related to the standing trustee’s accounts.  The standing trustee must provide an authorization for the bank’s release of information to the United States Trustee (See “Release” in the Supplemental Materials).  28 C.F.R. § 58.4(b) referencing 28 C.F.R. § 58.3(b)(7).

entries.title: 3.  	BANK ACCOUNTS
entries.content: 3.  	BANK ACCOUNTS

entries.title: The standing trustee must have a pre-confirmation ...
entries.content: The standing trustee must have a pre-confirmation trust account, a post-confirmation trust account, and an operating expense account.    The pre-confirmation and post-confirmation trust account may be combined into a single bank account provided that the standing trustee can adequately distinguish between pre- and post- confirmation funds.

entries.title: a.	Pre-confirmation Trust Funds
entries.content: a.	Pre-confirmation Trust Funds

entries.title: Pre-confirmation trust funds may not be deposited ...
entries.content: Pre-confirmation trust funds may not be deposited in an interest bearing account unless authorized by the United States Trustee.  Authorization will be given only if (i) the standing trustee’s accounting system is able to accurately allocate the interest earned by each debtor’s account, (ii) the standing trustee refunds all earned interest to a debtor if a debtor’s case is dismissed or converted prior to confirmation, (iii) the standing trustee transfers interest allocated to each debtor’s account to the operating expense account only upon confirmation, and (iv) the standing trustee issues IRS Form 1099’s to debtors receiving interest refunds of $10 or more.

entries.title: If the standing trustee maintains a separate pre-c...
entries.content: If the standing trustee maintains a separate pre-confirmation trust account, when the plan is confirmed the standing trustee must transfer the plan payments made by the debtor or on the debtor’s behalf (debtor payments) to the post-confirmation trust account, and transfer any interest earned on those debtor payments to the operating expense account.

entries.title: b. 	Post-confirmation Trust Funds
entries.content: b. 	Post-confirmation Trust Funds

entries.title: The standing trustee must make all disbursements u...
entries.content: The standing trustee must make all disbursements under confirmed plans from the post-confirmation trust account.  All interest earned by the account must be transferred to the operating expense account at least monthly.  If the standing trustee maintains a separate post-confirmation trust account, the standing trustee must transfer the debtor payments to the pre-confirmation trust account if the case is unconfirmed.  Transfer must be done on a regular basis, and no less than monthly.

entries.title: c.	Operating Expense Account
entries.content: c.	Operating Expense Account

entries.title: The standing trustee must deposit all percentage f...
entries.content: The standing trustee must deposit all percentage fee income, interest income, and other receipts not deposited in the pre- or post-confirmation accounts into the operating expense account.  The standing trustee must pay all compensation, operating expenses, and payments to the United States Trustee System Fund out of the operating expense account.  The standing trustee may not use the operating expense account for deposit or payment of any funds unrelated to the administration of the standing trustee’s chapter 13 cases.

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 345		Money of Estates
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 345		Money of Estates

entries.title: Practice Tips:
entries.content: Practice Tips:

entries.title: Disbursement accounts - in order to improve the se...
entries.content: Disbursement accounts - in order to improve the security of the trust funds, a disbursement account may be used, provided the account meets all trust account requirements.

entries.title: In order to improve the maximum interest earned on...
entries.content: In order to improve the maximum interest earned on confirmed trust funds prior to their disbursement, the standing trustee may transfer funds to FDIC-insured investment accounts or through direct purchase of U.S. government securities.

entries.title: To the extent that the standing trustee utilizes a...
entries.content: To the extent that the standing trustee utilizes a payroll service or automatic withdrawals from the operating expense account, procedures should be developed to ensure the security of the expense funds.  Those may include a separate payroll account or a block on the expense account to protect against unauthorized withdrawals by the payroll company.

entries.title: B.	BANK REQUIREMENTS
entries.content: B.	BANK REQUIREMENTS

entries.title: 1.	GENERAL
entries.content: 1.	GENERAL

entries.title: A standing trustee may deposit funds in a banking ...
entries.content: A standing trustee may deposit funds in a banking institution only if the bank agrees to the terms below.  If a bank holding a standing trustee’s funds fails to comply with any of these terms, the standing trustee must promptly notify the United States Trustee and promptly move the funds to a bank that complies with these terms.

entries.title: a.	Provide monthly bank statements in paper form t...
entries.content: a.	Provide monthly bank statements in paper form to the standing trustee and quarterly bank balance reports to the United States Trustee.

entries.title: b.		Provide canceled checks with the monthly bank ...
entries.content: b.		Provide canceled checks with the monthly bank statements.  Canceled checks may be in the form of: (1) the canceled original checks; (2) canceled substitute checks; (3) electronic images of both the front and back of each canceled check; or (4) any combination thereof.  Electronic images of canceled checks must be presented using one of two methods: (1) unalterable CDs, with the front and back of the canceled checks segregated by account, or (2) on paper with no more than four checks (front and back, eight images total) per statement page.  Electronic images of canceled checks in paper form must be printed on paper that is identifiable as coming from the depository, for example paper containing the depository’s logo.

entries.title: c.		Provide an annual statement detailing any pers...
entries.content: c.		Provide an annual statement detailing any personal financial relationship with the standing trustee and certifying that the standing trustee has not and will not receive favorable treatment inuring to the personal benefit of the standing trustee.

entries.title: d.		Comply with the collateral requirements more f...
entries.content: d.		Comply with the collateral requirements more fully described in the following section on Collateral.  11 U.S.C. § 345.

entries.title: e.	Provide the United States Trustee with the stan...
entries.content: e.	Provide the United States Trustee with the standing trustee’s bank records on request of the United States Trustee, and with written authorization of the standing trustee.  See “Release” in the Supplemental Materials.

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 12 U.S.C. §§ 5001-5018	Check Clearing For the 21st...
entries.content: 12 U.S.C. §§ 5001-5018	Check Clearing For the 21st Century Act

entries.title: 2. 	COLLATERAL
entries.content: 2. 	COLLATERAL

entries.title: A standing trustee may deposit funds only in a ban...
entries.content: A standing trustee may deposit funds only in a banking institution that agrees to collateralize all chapter 13 funds on deposit in excess of the FDIC insurance limitation.  11 U.S.C. § 345.  For purposes of the FDIC limitation, unless the United States Trustee approves an exemption, the standing trustee must aggregate the funds of individual debtors in the commingled trust accounts.  12 C.F.R. § 330.  The United States Trustee will approve the exemption from aggregation only if the United States Trustee receives the following written assurances:

entries.title: a.		The financial institution must provide written...
entries.content: a.		The financial institution must provide written evidence that its deposit account records specifically disclose the existence of a fiduciary relationship.  Words such as “trust account” or “trustee” must be used.  To verify that account records continue to reflect the existence of a fiduciary relationship, the depository institution shall be required to provide an affidavit to the United States Trustee on an annual basis.  12 C.F.R. § 330.5.

entries.title: The standing trustee must provide written evidence...
entries.content: The standing trustee must provide written evidence that records are maintained in the regular course of business which reflect the interest of each debtor in the commingled funds deposited to the trust accounts and that each of the respective interests is less than the FDIC insurance limitation. Although all funds for a particular debtor will ordinarily be maintained in one account and in a single financial institution, a standing trustee may occasionally hold debtor funds in multiple accounts or in a certificate of deposit.  To ensure the FDIC insurance limit is not exceeded, the United States Trustee also must be able to ascertain from the standing trustee’s records, the amount and location of all funds held on account for each estate, including funds deposited into separate trust accounts or certificates of deposit. The FDIC insurance limit applies per financial institution to the interest of each single debtor even if the debtor’s funds are in more than one account.  While there is no prescribed way to provide the written evidence, it is preferable that the standing trustee maintain a computer-generated report which identifies each case and the amount held on account for the case, and demonstrates that the report has been reconciled with the last monthly bank statement(s).  12 C.F.R. § 330.13.

entries.title: c.	The standing trustee must notify the bank and t...
entries.content: c.	The standing trustee must notify the bank and the United States Trustee if a debtor’s aggregate interest in the funds on deposit in a single bank exceeds the FDIC insurance limitation so that the bank can provide additional collateralization.  The standing trustee also must notify the bank and the United States Trustee if the interest in the funds on deposit attributed to the suspense funds or to the expense account exceed the FDIC insurance limitation.  11 U.S.C. § 345.

entries.title: The financial institution may post a surety bond i...
entries.content: The financial institution may post a surety bond in lieu of collateralizing chapter 13 accounts.  The bond must be approved by the United States Trustee.  11 U.S.C. § 345.

entries.title: If the bank fails to comply with the collateral re...
entries.content: If the bank fails to comply with the collateral requirements of the Bankruptcy Code and more fully detailed in this Handbook, or fails to enter into the most recent “Uniform Depository Agreement” between the bank and the United States Trustee, the United States Trustee shall notify the standing trustee.

entries.title: If the bank fails to provide the United States Tru...
entries.content: If the bank fails to provide the United States Trustee with any bank records, documents, and information related to the standing trustee’s accounts on request of the United States Trustee after receiving the standing trustee’s written consent, the United Stated Trustee shall notify the standing trustee.

entries.title: Related Provisions:
entries.content: Related Provisions:

entries.title: 11 U.S.C. § 345		Money of Estates
entries.content: 11 U.S.C. § 345		Money of Estates

entries.title: 12 C.F.R. § 330		Deposit Insurance Coverage
entries.content: 12 C.F.R. § 330		Deposit Insurance Coverage

entries.title: C.  	FINANCIAL RECORD KEEPING AND REPORTING
entries.content: C.  	FINANCIAL RECORD KEEPING AND REPORTING

entries.title: 1.	GENERAL CHARACTERISTICS
entries.content: 1.	GENERAL CHARACTERISTICS

entries.title: The standing trustee’s financial record keeping an...
entries.content: The standing trustee’s financial record keeping and reporting system must reflect accurately the results of operation.  The systems must have the following characteristics:

entries.title: a.	Financial records must be updated daily and ref...
entries.content: a.	Financial records must be updated daily and reflect each day’s activities separate from that of another day.  The records must show the financial position as of a certain date.

entries.title: b.	Financial records must be stored in secure faci...
entries.content: b.	Financial records must be stored in secure facilities.  Records must be protected from fire and natural disaster.

entries.title: c.	Subsidiary records must be reconciled periodica...
entries.content: c.	Subsidiary records must be reconciled periodically with general ledger account balances.

entries.title: d.	The financial record keeping system must allow ...
entries.content: d.	The financial record keeping system must allow the standing trustee or auditor to trace and verify transactions.

entries.title: e.	The financial record keeping system must facili...
entries.content: e.	The financial record keeping system must facilitate preparation of internal reports that will assist the standing trustee in the performance of duties.

entries.title: f.	Financial records may be kept in either paper o...
entries.content: f.	Financial records may be kept in either paper or an electronic format.

entries.title: 2.	ACCOUNTING RECORDS
entries.content: 2.	ACCOUNTING RECORDS

entries.title: A standing trustee’s accounting records must be au...
entries.content: A standing trustee’s accounting records must be automated and contain the following:

entries.title: a.	General Ledger – a detailed record of deposits ...
entries.content: a.	General Ledger – a detailed record of deposits and expenditures of the standing trustee’s trust and expense accounts broken down by category.

entries.title: b.	Cash Receipts Journal – list of all funds recei...
entries.content: b.	Cash Receipts Journal – list of all funds received including funds from or on behalf of debtors and funds from creditors.

entries.title: Cash Disbursements Journal – list of all disbursem...
entries.content: Cash Disbursements Journal – list of all disbursements from the trust accounts.

entries.title: Individual Case Cash Receipts and Disbursements Le...
entries.content: Individual Case Cash Receipts and Disbursements Ledgers – listing of receipts and disbursements in each individual debtor’s account.

entries.title: Fixed Asset Register (Inventory) – a subsidiary ac...
entries.content: Fixed Asset Register (Inventory) – a subsidiary account listing all items of property which exceed $500 in cost and have a useful life of at least one year.  Upon disposition of assets, the standing trustee must delete items from the register and identify their disposition. All assets must be listed with a unique serial number or an identification number.  The standing trustee must submit a copy of the Fixed Asset Register to the United States Trustee as of the end of each fiscal year with the Annual Report.

entries.title: Practice Tips:
entries.content: Practice Tips:

entries.title: 1.	The standing trustee should prepare at year-end...
entries.content: 1.	The standing trustee should prepare at year-end a summary showing beginning fixed asset inventory balance, additions, deletions and ending inventory balance, and a detailed listing that agrees with the ending inventory balance.  This will assist the auditors in their reconciliation.

entries.title: 2.	Fixed assets could be bar coded to assist in tr...
entries.content: 2.	Fixed assets could be bar coded to assist in tracking.

entries.title: 3.	ACCOUNTING REPORTS
entries.content: 3.	ACCOUNTING REPORTS

entries.title: a.	Final Reports
entries.content: a.	Final Reports

entries.title: Subject to Rule 1019, the standing trustee must pr...
entries.content: Subject to Rule 1019, the standing trustee must promptly file a final report and account for each chapter 13 debtor after final distribution and after all checks relating to the case have cleared the bank.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(9).  Generally, the final report should be filed within 150 days of the last disbursement in the case.  The standing trustee must use UST Form 101-13-FR-S.  28 U.S.C. § 589b, 28 C.F.R. § 58.7.

entries.title: The report must show the total of all receipts fro...
entries.content: The report must show the total of all receipts from the debtor and total disbursements made to each creditor by the standing trustee during the reporting period.  28 U.S.C. § 589b.

entries.title: b.  	Periodic Debtor Reports
entries.content: b.  	Periodic Debtor Reports

entries.title: The standing trustee must mail, at least annually,...
entries.content: The standing trustee must mail, at least annually, to the debtor and debtor’s attorney, a report showing all receipts from the debtor and all disbursements made during the reporting period.   The report also must show the amount of undistributed funds on hand.  This report may be sent by electronic mail.  The standing trustee must maintain a copy of these reports or certify to the United States Trustee that the reports have been sent.  11 U.S.C. § 1302(b)(1) incorporating 11 U.S.C. § 704(a)(7), 28 C.F.R. § 58.4(b) incorporating 28 C.F.R. § 58.3(b)(7).

entries.title: Practice Tips:
entries.content: Practice Tips:

entries.title: 1.	These reports may be outsourced for efficiency.
entries.content: 1.	These reports may be outsourced for efficiency.

entries.title: 2. 	If not outsourced, reports to the same debtors...
entries.content: 2. 	If not outsourced, reports to the same debtors’ attorney may be burned to a CD and sent to that attorney.

entries.title: 3.	Debtors or their counsel may be more likely to ...
entries.content: 3.	Debtors or their counsel may be more likely to notice errors if reports are sent every six months.

entries.title: D.	INTERNAL FINANCIAL CONTROLS
entries.content: D.	INTERNAL FINANCIAL CONTROLS

entries.title: The standing trustee must be actively and personal...
entries.content: The standing trustee must be actively and personally involved in establishing and maintaining a system of internal controls to safeguard trust funds and property, to ensure the integrity of financial records, and to discourage employee theft.

entries.title: The system of internal controls must include at le...
entries.content: The system of internal controls must include at least the following components:

entries.title: 1.	PERSONNEL MANAGEMENT
entries.content: 1.	PERSONNEL MANAGEMENT

entries.title: a.  	The standing trustee must actively supervise ...
entries.content: a.  	The standing trustee must actively supervise employees in the performance of their cash management and accounting duties.

entries.title: b.	The standing trustee must have written employee...
entries.content: b.	The standing trustee must have written employee job descriptions.

entries.title: c.	The standing trustee must document routine staf...
entries.content: c.	The standing trustee must document routine staff procedures.

entries.title: d.	The standing trustee must maintain and review r...
entries.content: d.	The standing trustee must maintain and review records of employee’s time and attendance and must document paid overtime.

entries.title: e.	The standing trustee must implement a vacation ...
entries.content: e.	The standing trustee must implement a vacation policy which provides that employees be absent from their duties for an uninterrupted period of not less than one week.  To provide an effective internal control, another employee must assume the duties performed by the absent individual.

entries.title: Practice Tips:
entries.content: Practice Tips:

entries.title: 1.	The standing trustee should be aware of the emp...
entries.content: 1.	The standing trustee should be aware of the employee fraud indicators provided by the United States Trustee as listed in the Supplemental Materials.

entries.title: 2.	The standing trustee should consider conducting...
entries.content: 2.	The standing trustee should consider conducting background checks and check with prior employers prior to the hiring of employees.   The standing trustees may use an outside agency to conduct any background checks on employee applicants. A background check by an outside agency may include such items as employee reference checks, credit checks, and public record searches. However, any background check should be conducted only with the written

entries.title: consent of the applicant and should be in complian...
entries.content: consent of the applicant and should be in compliance with applicable State and Federal laws.

entries.title: 2.	SEGREGATION OF DUTIES
entries.content: 2.	SEGREGATION OF DUTIES

entries.title: To the extent possible, the standing trustee must ...
entries.content: To the extent possible, the standing trustee must assign employees tasks in only one of the following categories of job duties: (1) physical control over assets, i.e., handling of receipts, returned checks or any other item than can be reduced to cash; (2) bookkeeping, i.e., posting or accumulating either computer or manual records and transactions; or (3) reconciling account records and transactions, i.e., bank reconciliations, suspense accounts, etc.

entries.title: As much as is practicable, the standing trustee mu...
entries.content: As much as is practicable, the standing trustee must arrange duties of the trustee operation so that no one person is responsible for any transaction from beginning to end:

entries.title: a.	The standing trustee must be actively involved ...
entries.content: a.	The standing trustee must be actively involved in the process of issuing all checks, authorizing stop payments, and reviewing the reconciliation of bank statements.

entries.title: b.	When small staff size precludes segregation of ...
entries.content: b.	When small staff size precludes segregation of duties, the standing trustee must be actively involved in that duty.

entries.title: c.	The standing trustee must separate cash handlin...
entries.content: c.	The standing trustee must separate cash handling duties from record keeping and reconciliation functions whenever possible.

entries.title: d.	Whenever possible, employees involved in the pr...
entries.content: d.	Whenever possible, employees involved in the preparation of trust and expense disbursement checks must not be involved in the mailing process.

entries.title: e.	Whenever possible, employees involved in proces...
entries.content: e.	Whenever possible, employees involved in processing returned disbursement checks must not be involved with initial check authorization, preparation, or bookkeeping.

entries.title: f.	Whenever possible, employees involved in the re...
entries.content: f.	Whenever possible, employees involved in the reconcilement process must not have check disbursement responsibilities.

entries.title: g.	The standing trustee must open and review expen...
entries.content: g.	The standing trustee must open and review expense account bank statements and canceled checks prior to the bank account reconcilement by other staff.

entries.title: h.	When a check printer or facsimile check signing...
entries.content: h.	When a check printer or facsimile check signing machine is used, the standing trustee must establish procedures designed to overcome the absence of direct signer involvement.  Procedures might include sole control of the signature cartridge or signature plate by the standing trustee, or restricting access to check printing.  The standing trustee must have a procedure in place to secure or lock the signature cartridge, plate, chip or machine.
